whyHot: The single most-recommended first bass shaker of 2026 across nearly every roundup — ~1,000 reviews, ~80% 5-star, named the best balance of price and 50W punch that guides tell newcomers to buy first. Works under a seat for racing, a flight bench, or a space/marine chair.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has ONE haptic item (ButtKicker Gamer PRO) and zero shakers. Tactile is the #1 immersion upgrade the whole community pushes after the hero peripherals — and it applies to EVERY bay (racing seat, flight rig, space chair, marine helm). Needs its own product + a 'how tactile/SimHub works' explainer.

whyHot: 2026 'Editor's Choice' for maximum immersion — 300W RMS delivering defined bass from subtle road/engine texture to hard hits; reviewers call it a smarter buy than the ButtKicker Mini LFE with more power and a lower price.
notesForCatalog: The step-up tactile unit for builders who outgrow the BST-1. Pairs with a Fosi amp. Gives the catalog a clean 'entry vs. endgame' tactile upgrade path story.

whyHot: The cheapest entry into tactile at ~$15-25 per unit; repeatedly named the budget pick because you can wire several in series/parallel to spread feedback across pedals, seat, and yoke deck.
notesForCatalog: Perfect 'try tactile for the price of lunch' entry, and a great multi-puck localized-feedback story (front puck vs rear puck). Cheap, high-review, universal across all bays.

whyHot: The 2026 go-to amp for driving bass shakers — ~$145, up to 5 channels so you can run localized 4.1/5.0 tactile zones, with Bluetooth/AUX/optical inputs. Every shaker guide now points buyers here as the companion purchase.
notesForCatalog: Shakers are useless without an amp — the catalog needs the amp to complete the tactile bundle. Multi-channel HT4S lets one amp feed every bay's rig. High-intent 'what amp do I need' searches convert well.

product: Next Level Racing HF8 Haptic Gaming Pad
whyHot: The community's plug-and-play tactile answer — 8 motors in a seat pad, SimHub-driven, and QUIET, so apartment builders who can't run bass shakers still get feedback. Reviewers call it 'affordable haptic feedback' and it drops onto any chair or rig.
notesForCatalog: The no-wiring, no-amp tactile option — sits between 'no tactile' and 'shaker + amp build.' Universal: works on a racing seat, flight chair, marine helm seat. Strong 'renter / no-DIY' recommendation.

whyHot: The evergreen 'exceptional value' pick for space-constrained setups at ~$230 — patented foldable design that stores flat. Repeatedly the default first-seat recommendation for apartment and shared-room builders across racing AND flight.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has ZERO seating. Seat/cockpit is the load-bearing foundation of every bay and one of the most-searched first buys. Foldable Challenge covers the huge 'I don't have a dedicated room' audience.

whyHot: One of the cheapest genuine FIA-approved bucket seats and the community's go-to for a real racing feel; the standard 'upgrade your Playseat with a real bucket' recommendation. Bolstered support that also suits flight and space builders who want a wrap-around seat.
notesForCatalog: The 'endgame seat' half of the seating story (vs. the foldable Playseat entry). Side-mount pattern fits most aluminum-profile rigs across bays. Real-bucket-seat search intent is high.

whyHot: Named the top long-session chair in 2026 seat guides for comfort/ergonomics and a 12-year warranty — the pick for flight-sim, space-sim, and desk-based builders who sit for hours and don't want a bucket seat. The 'grail chair' the community keeps recommending for marathon sessions.
notesForCatalog: Not every builder wants a bucket seat — flight/space/desk sim users often want a premium ergonomic chair. Covers the 'comfort-first, non-racing' seating audience the catalog totally misses.

whyHot: Featured in Forbes' 'Best Gaming Chair for Serious Gamers' list; the accessible ~$130-345 pick guides point newcomers to for proper ergonomics without racing-aesthetic gimmicks. The default 'first real chair' recommendation.
notesForCatalog: The budget seating entry point. High Amazon volume + affiliate-friendly price band. Universal across all desk-based sim bays.

whyHot: The 2026 default single-screen wraparound — a 49" 32:9 1800R OLED gives the horizontal FOV of a triple-27" setup with no bezels, one cable, and one GPU output. Repeatedly the most-recommended 'best of both worlds' display for racing and flight.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has only 2 monitors (one 49" Samsung + one 27" ASUS). Displays are a top-3 spend for every bay yet barely represented. The 49" ultrawide is the single most-recommended immersive screen of 2026.

whyHot: The world's first 57" dual-UHD (7680x2160) display and the 2026 halo screen — the 'money is no object' single-monitor endgame for triple-replacement immersion in racing and flight sims.
notesForCatalog: The aspirational top of the display ladder — great high-ticket affiliate anchor and a natural 'endgame display' article. Needs an RTX 5090 to drive it (cross-sell).

whyHot: Named the best ultrawide for iRacing in 2026 — a 45" 21:9 OLED with an aggressive 800R wrap that deep-curves around your vision. The enthusiast alternative to the 49" Samsung for racers who want the tightest curve.
notesForCatalog: Gives the display lineup a second premium-OLED voice (LG vs Samsung) so buyers have a real comparison. Strong for the 'best monitor for iRacing' search cluster.

whyHot: The most-recommended budget free-standing triple stand — stable, VESA, no drilling, and the default answer when someone asks how to mount three screens without a cockpit. Universal to any desk or rig.
notesForCatalog: Monitors need a stand — a category the catalog has zero of. Cheap, Amazon-native, high-attach-rate with every triple or ultrawide purchase. Applies to every desk-based bay.

product: Advanced SimRacing Free-Standing Triple Ultrawide Stand (up to 45")
whyHot: The premium free-standing stand rated for 45" ultrawides and big TVs (~$840); the community's go-to when a VIVO can't hold a 49"+ panel. Named the premium option in free-standing mounting guides.
notesForCatalog: The heavy-duty half of the mounting story — pairs with the big 49"/57" OLEDs the catalog should carry. Solves the 'my ultrawide is too heavy for a cheap stand' problem.

whyHot: Named the best-value budget VR headset of 2026 at ~$349 — same Horizon OS library, pancake lenses, works standalone or as PCVR via Link/Air Link. The most-recommended 'first VR headset' entry point for racing and flight.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has Quest 3, Pimax Crystal Light, and a marine Quest 3 — but not the cheapest on-ramp. The 3S is THE budget entry recommendation and where most VR-curious buyers actually start.

whyHot: The 2026 resolution benchmark — highest pixel count available, and named a top flight-sim headset for instrument readability. The 'maximum clarity, money is no object' pick racers and pilots aspire to.
notesForCatalog: The endgame VR anchor above the Crystal Light already in-catalog. Cross-sells with an RTX 5090. Completes the VR ladder: Quest 3S -> Crystal Light -> Crystal Super.

whyHot: The 2026 comfort/weight champion — widely called one of the most comfortable lightweight PCVR headsets for long simulation sessions. The pick when marathon comfort matters more than wireless.
notesForCatalog: Third VR voice with a distinct 'featherweight for endurance racing / long-haul flights' angle. Distinct enough from Pimax to earn its own slot and comparison content.

whyHot: The 2026 'default' sim headset recommendation — wireless, comfortable, 360 spatial audio for positional awareness, and simultaneous 2.4GHz game + Bluetooth Discord for league comms. The one the community tells everyone to just buy.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has zero audio. A spatial-audio headset is universal to every bay (positional awareness in racing, ATC/comms in flight, immersion in space). Dual-connect for league/Discord is the killer feature buyers search for.

whyHot: The evergreen budget classic still recommended in 2026 for comfort, durability, and 'good enough' virtual surround with Windows Sonic. The safe, cheap, universally-liked wired pick.
notesForCatalog: The budget audio entry beneath the Arctis Nova 7. Extremely high Amazon volume and a low-risk affiliate staple that fits every bay and every budget.

product: Philips Hue Play Light Bars + Sync
whyHot: Boosted Media's 'next-level immersion for under $300' pick — Hue Sync mirrors on-screen color into the room in real time, adding depth and reducing eye strain. The most-cited premium adaptive-lighting solution for sim rigs.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has zero lighting. Adaptive bias lighting is a huge immersion/aesthetic upgrade that applies to any screen-based bay. Hue = the accurate-color premium voice; strong 'immersion for under $300' article hook.

whyHot: The value king of RGB strips and bars — reasonably priced across the board, screen-sync capable, and the community's most-mounted sim-rig light-bar (dedicated rig mounts even exist on Etsy). The default budget ambient pick.
notesForCatalog: The budget lighting entry beneath Hue. Amazon-native, huge review counts, and universal to any rig or battlestation. Pairs with an 'adaptive lighting on a budget' angle.

product: Sim Racing Studio Hurricane PRO Wind Kit
whyHot: The premium telemetry-driven wind kit of 2026 — fan speed syncs to virtual velocity for real speed sensation plus cooling. Guides call wind 'a legitimate performance tool' for endurance, triples, and especially VR (cuts headset heat).
notesForCatalog: A genuinely trending immersion category with zero catalog presence. Doubles as cooling for VR/endurance users. SimHub-driven so it ties into the same tactile/telemetry story. Applies to racing, flight, and space bays.

product: Apex Sim Racing 8-Outlet Sim Rig Mounted Power Strip
whyHot: The community's go-to for taming a rig's rat's-nest of plugs — 8 outlets that clamp to 40x40/80x20 aluminum profile with 2100J surge protection and a 15A breaker. Recommended to shield sensitive direct-drive/motion electronics from grid fluctuations.
notesForCatalog: Zero power products in catalog. Every multi-device rig (DD base + monitor + pedals + shakers + fans) needs consolidated, protected power. Rig-mounted design is the clean-build answer and applies to every bay.

whyHot: Recommended for motion and direct-drive rigs to ride out brownouts and dirty power that can trip or EMI-corrupt DD/actuator electronics — a battery + surge + power-conditioning layer the community points to for expensive rigs.
notesForCatalog: Underrated protection for pricey DD/motion rigs — a clean 'protect your $2,000 base' article. High-ticket affiliate item that most builders don't know they need until a brownout scares them.

product: Honeycomb Flight Sim USB Hub (7-port powered, 60W)
whyHot: The community answer to the #1 home-cockpit pain point — running out of powered USB for panels, yokes, throttles, and button boxes. A powered 7-port/60W hub that reliably drives Honeycomb AND WinWing gear where unpowered hubs drop out.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has one generic 10-port hub but not the flight-specific powered-hub story. Multi-device rigs (flight panels, button boxes, shakers, wind, tablet) all need powered USB. High-attach with any panel purchase.

whyHot: The most-recommended value powered hub in flight-sim cockpit threads — 7 data ports + 2 smart-charging ports, reliably powers WinWing/Honeycomb panels without brownouts. The 'just buy this' budget alternative to brand hubs.
notesForCatalog: The budget/cross-platform hub voice beside the Honeycomb one. Amazon-native, high review count, universal to any USB-heavy rig (flight panels, sim-racing button boxes, space HOTAS + MFDs).

whyHot: The default r/battlestations / clean-build cable-taming kit — neoprene split sleeves, velcro ties, and adhesive clips that tidy the wiring behind any rig. The universally-recommended cheap 'make your rig look pro' upgrade.
notesForCatalog: Zero cable-management in catalog despite it being the single most-common 'clean up your rig' recommendation. Cheap, high-attach, universal, and the natural companion to the power-strip entry. Great 'finish the build' content.

product: Next Level Racing Elite Keyboard and Mouse Tray
whyHot: The top rig-mounted keyboard/mouse solution of 2026 — height/angle/articulation-adjustable anodized aluminum, so you can chat/menu without breaking cockpit position. The go-to answer for 'where does my keyboard go.'
notesForCatalog: A universal ergonomics gap — every seated rig needs somewhere for a keyboard/mouse (menus, SimHub, ATC text, chat). Applies to racing, flight, and space cockpits equally.

product: Next Level Racing Elite Tablet/Button Box Mount
whyHot: The most-recommended way to mount a tablet or phone as a SimHub dash / secondary display (or a button box) on an aluminum-profile rig — 3 mounting variations, fits Elite and other 8020 cockpits. Turns a spare tablet into a live telemetry dash.
notesForCatalog: Enables the huge 'use my old tablet/phone as a SimHub dash' DIY trend for free. Universal: racing dash, flight EFB/charts, space MFD. Cheap add-on with high attach to any rig.

whyHot: The 2026 consensus sweet-spot GPU — the standard pick for triple-1440p iRacing (holds 120Hz on a full grid) and the realistic minimum for high-fidelity flight in VR (Crystal Light / Beyond 2 at sensible PPD). The card guides tell most builders to target.
notesForCatalog: Zero compute in catalog, yet the PC/GPU is the invisible foundation that decides whether every peripheral even runs well. The 5080 is the single most-recommended sim GPU — natural 'what PC do I need' pillar content across all bays.

whyHot: The 2026 endgame card — what guides say you need for 4K triples, the 57" dual-UHD, or a Pimax Crystal Super, and for 4K DCS in VR. The 'no compromise' halo GPU that anchors the most demanding builds.
notesForCatalog: The high-ticket compute anchor that cross-sells with the 57" Neo G9 and Crystal Super. Completes the 'GPU by resolution/headset' decision content.

whyHot: Called out as the value standout among premium RTX 5080 prebuilts in 2026 — Ryzen 9 + RTX 5080 delivering proper 4K without 5090 pricing. The recommended turnkey pick for builders who won't assemble their own.
notesForCatalog: Serves the large 'I don't want to build a PC' audience. A turnkey prebuilt is a high-ticket affiliate item and the honest answer for many first-time sim buyers across every bay.

whyHot: The 2026 value darling — 12Nm at ~$429/$799 depending on region, a price point 'the rest of the 12Nm market struggles to match.' The most-recommended mid-tier DD base and the entry to MOZA's fast-growing ecosystem.
notesForCatalog: Catalog already carries the MOZA line (R5/R9/R12/R16/R21). Listed here only as a trending signal — verify the R12 'V2' revision and 2026 price are reflected. NOT a diversity gap; the base category is already deep.

whyHot: May 2026 firmware unlocked +3Nm, pushing the DD+ to 18Nm; tested on par with Simucube 2 Pro and Asetek Forte for responsiveness, and it's the DD base with PS5 compatibility. Big 2026 talking point in every Fanatec-vs-MOZA thread.
notesForCatalog: Catalog already has Fanatec ClubSport DD/DD+. Listed as a trending signal only — update the DD+ spec to 18Nm post-firmware and note PS5 support. NOT a diversity gap.

whyHot: The most-recommended safe screen/optics cleaner in gaming/VR communities — ammonia-free, safe for OLED coatings and VR lenses, and the go-to for keeping big OLED ultrawides and headset lenses smudge-free. Cheap, universal upkeep item.
notesForCatalog: Zero maintenance products in catalog. An expensive OLED ultrawide or VR headset needs a coating-safe cleaner — an easy 'protect your investment' add-on with universal appeal and a natural attach to displays/VR.
